data.title: Parsons' Growth Story Falls Short When Compared To Peers
data.description:
Investment thesis

Parsons Corporation (NYSE:PSN) has delivered strong top-line growth over the past 8 years. But this was driven by acquisitions. However, this growth has not translated into superior returns compared to its peers. It ranked near the bottom among peers in EBIT margin, return on capital, and free cash flow. Despite improved cost control, I have concerns about its unsustainable reinvestment rate. My valuation also did not provide any margin of safety. As such, Parsons is not a compelling value opportunity.

Business background

Parsons is a consulting company and an engineering contractor, but with a strong tilt toward technology-driven defense, intelligence, and infrastructure solutions. Its business is organized into two main segments:

  • Federal Solutions. This segment delivers high-end technology and mission-critical services primarily to US government agencies.
  • Critical Infrastructure. This segment focuses on modernizing physical and digital infrastructure.

The company IPOed in 2019. In the initial years of its public-listed life, the Critical Infrastructure segment was the biggest revenue contributor. However, by 2024, the Federal Solutions segment had overtaken it. Refer to the left part of Chart 1.

With the Federal Solutions segment as an important revenue contributor, you should not be surprised that North America accounted for the bulk of the company’s total revenue. Refer to the right part of Chart 1.

Looking at its business profile, I would describe Parsons as serving the US defense and global civil engineering markets. These are single-digit growth markets:

“The US Defense Market Size is estimated at USD 320.86 billion in 2025…at a CAGR of 3.58% during the forecast period (2025 – 2030)” Mordor Intelligence.

“The global Civil Engineering Market is…Valued at USD 9,162.7 million in 2023… growing at a 5.3% CAGR from 2024 to 2033.” Market.US

Acquisition-driven revenue growth

While serving low-single-digit markets, Parsons has been able to deliver revenue growth of 12.2% CAGR from 2017 to 2024. A big part of this was due to the more than a dozen acquisitions it has undertaken since 2018. These have enabled it to leapfrog into high-tech verticals, especially for the US defense and intelligence communities.

To give you a sense of the acquisitions, from 2017 to 2025, Parsons spent about USD 2.5 billion on acquisitions compared to USD 0.3 billion on CAPEX.

Looking at Chart 1, you can see that the revenues for 2023 and 2024 were substantially higher than those pre-2023. Revenue growth rates were 30% and 24% for 2023 and 2024, respectively. The company attributed these to the high organic growth rates.

“The increase in Federal Solutions revenue for… 2023…was primarily due to organic growth of 25%...The increase in Critical Infrastructure revenue for…2023…was substantially due to organic growth.” 2023 Form 10K

“The increase in Federal Solutions revenue for… 2024…was primarily related to organic growth of 30%...The increase in Critical Infrastructure revenue for… 2024 was primarily related to organic growth of 12%...” 2024 Form 10K.

Parsons' high organic growth in 2023–2024 was driven by a unique convergence of contract timing, government stimulus, and strong execution. As mentioned earlier, Parsons operates in single-digit growth sectors.

As such, I would not expect the double-digit revenue growth rate due to organic growth to continue. In this context, the company, in its Q1 2025 Presentation, provided guidance of 5% for organic growth.

Moving forward, I would expect the company to continue with its acquisition-led growth path. The company alluded to this in its Q1 2025 presentation.

"Parsons to continue to make internal investments and accretive acquisitions to drive additional growth.”

Growth needs to be funded on one metric for this is the Reinvestment.

Reinvestment = CAPEX & Acquisition – Depreciation & Amortization + increase in Net Working Capital

From 2017 to 2022, Parsons had an average Reinvestment rate (Reinvestment/NOPAT) of 181%. This is an unsustainable rate. Excluding the acquisitions, the average Reinvestment rate was negative. The negative rate was because the Depreciation & Amortization more than offset the CAPEX for many years.

Neither the greater than 100% nor the negative rates are sustainable rates. Over the long run, I would expect the Reinvestment rate to be governed by the fundamental growth equation.

Growth = Return X Reinvestment rate.

Based on the past 2 years' average Return (ROIC) of 10% and the projected 7% 2025 growth rate, I would expect the Reinvestment rate to go down to below 70%.

Improving operating trends

While revenue grew at 12.2% from 2017 to 2018, PAT grew at 14.7% CAGR. This slightly better profit growth rate was due more to higher revenue and better cost control rather than margin expansion.

You can see from the right part of Chart 2 that while there was some uptrend in the gross profit margin, it peaked in 2021 and trended down thereafter. This is reflected in the contribution margin trend. Refer to the left part of Chart 2.

I think that 2021 seemed to be an outlier, as exemplified by the following quotes from the 2021 and 2024 Forms 10k:

“…27.8% of our operating income during fiscal 2021… was derived from equity in our unconsolidated joint ventures.”

“…5.5% of our operating income during fiscal 2024, 16.6%...during fiscal 2023, and 8.8%... during fiscal 2022 was derived from equity in our unconsolidated joint ventures…”

However, the Selling, General and Administration (SGA) margins seemed to have a stronger downtrend post-2021. This is reflected by the declining fixed cost margin that averaged 17% for 2023/24 compared to 18% for 2017/18. In other words, fixed costs did not grow as fast as revenue.

Notes to Chart 2:

I broke down the operating profits into fixed costs and variable costs.

  • Fixed cost = SGA and Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Variable cost = Cost of Sales – Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Contribution = Revenue – Variable Cost.
  • Contribution margin = Contribution/Revenue.

The better profit performance translated into better returns.

  • ROIC averaged 10% for 2023/24 compared to the average 4% for the first 2 years of the IPO (2019/20).
  • ROE averaged 10% for 2023/24 compared to the 7% average for 2019/20.

These past 2 years' returns far exceeded their cost of funds, suggesting value was created for the shareholders as the company grew bigger.

Peer comparison

I compared Parsons' performance with the following listed companies that served similar markets:

  • AECOM (ACM) This is a global infrastructure consulting firm that delivers architecture, engineering, and project management services for transportation, environmental, and urban development projects worldwide.
  • Tetra Tech (TTEK). Tetra Tech is a leading provider of high-end consulting and engineering services focused on water, environment, sustainable infrastructure, and energy management.
  • CACI International (CACI). CACI delivers IT modernization, cybersecurity, and advanced intelligence solutions to US defense, intelligence, and federal civilian agencies.
  • Leidos Holdings (LDOS). Leidos is a science and technology company providing mission-critical solutions in defense, healthcare, civil infrastructure, and cybersecurity for the US government.

Parsons is a mid-sized player among its peers in terms of 2024 revenue, but ranked No. 1 in terms of growth rate. Refer to Table 1.

You can see that despite operating in single-digit growth sectors, most of the peers delivered double-digit growth rates. Note that TTEK, CACI and LDOS growths were also driven by acquisitions.

I compared Parsons' performance with that of its peers based on the following metrics. Refer to Charts 3 and 4. Overall, I would rank Parsons’ performance as below average.

  • Parsons ranked at the bottom for the return on capital.
  • While Parsons' EBIT margin has been trending up, so have those of the peers. As such, Parsons continued to deliver one of the worst EBIT margins.
  • I would also rate Parsons' unlevered free cash flow margin as one of the worst.
  • In terms of quantum, I would consider Parsons EPS as average. But it ranked No. 4 in terms of EPS growth rate.

Financial Position

I would consider Parsons as moderately sound financially, as its positive points offset its negative concerns. Its positive points are:

As of the end Dec. 2024, it had USD 453 million in cash, equal to about 8% of its total assets.

Over the past 8 years, it generated positive cash flow from operations every year. During this period, it had a good cash flow conversion rate, generating a total of USD 2.4 billion in cash flow from operations compared to the total PAT of 1.3 billion.

My concerns were:

It did not have a good capital allocation plan, as shown in Table 2. You can see that the cash flow from operations was not enough to fund its CAPEX and acquisitions. It has to raise additional capital.

It has a DE ratio of 56% as of the end of Dec 2024, the highest post-IPO.

It had an unsustainable Reinvestment rate. The key issue was the aggressive acquisitions and the funding for this. But this is within management control and can be brought to a sustainable Reinvestment rate.

Valuation

My analysis showed that Parsons has reached a size where it can deliver value, creating growth and profits. As such, I adopted the following picture in valuing Parsons.

  • The base revenue would be the 2025 revenue growing at 7% in Year 1. I adopted the 2025 management guidance for this. The growth rate would reduce proportionately to the 4% terminal growth rate in Year 6.
  • The base contribution margin would be the average 2017 to 2024 values and remain unchanged during the valuation period.
  • The base capita efficiency would be the average 2023 to 2024 values and remain unchanged during the valuation period.
  • The base Reinvestment rate would be the average 2023 to 2024 values and reduced proportionately to one based on the fundamental growth equation in the terminal year.
  • The fixed cost would be pegged to the average 2023 to 2024 fixed cost margin to reflect the cost improvements achieved.
  • The tax rate would be the post-IPO average tax rate of 23% (excluding the tax credit of 2019).

On such a basis, I obtained an intrinsic value of USD 55 per share compared to its market price (21 May 2025) of USD 67 per share. There is no margin of safety.

Valuation model

I valued Parsons based on a multi-stage Free Cash Flow to the Firm (FCFF) model, as shown in Table 3.

The basic equations used in the model are:

FCFF = EBIT(1 – t) – Reinvestment.

EBIT = Revenue X Contribution margin – Fixed cost

The contribution margin and fixed cost margin are based on the operating model shown in the right part of Chart 2.

The Reinvestment was based on the Reinvestment margin.

The fixed cost was based on the fixed cost margin.

Notes to Table 3:

a. Proportionate reduction

b. Pegged to the growth rate

c. Assumed no improvement

d. Revenue X fixed cost margin

e. Revenue X Margin, and after accounting for Fixed costs

f. Assumed proportionate improvement

g. b X f

h. FCFF for each year = e - g

j. Assumed constant D/E ratio. Refer to the WACC table

k. NPV for each year = (h X j)

l. Terminal for the year discounted at a terminal growth rate

m. 5 years NPV + terminal value

n. Inclusive of any excess TCE. Non-operating assets, MI, and Debt

o. Based on the number of shares

The WACC used in the valuation model was based on the first page results of a Google search for “Parsons WACC”. Refer to Table 4.

Risks and limitations

My valuation was based on a conservative revenue growth assumption. I assumed that revenue growth for Year 1 would be 7% as per the Q1 2025 guidance and would be reduced proportionately to the terminal growth rate of 4% in Year 6. This would result in a CAGR of 5.5% over the valuation period. This is much lower than the historical growth rate of 12.2% CAGR.

If I assumed that the average growth rate for the valuation period was the historical CAGR, I would have an intrinsic value of USD 97 per share. Note that this value assumes that the Reinvestment rate in the terminal year reduces to one given by the growth equation.

I will leave it to you to decide whether it makes sense to challenge management guidance on the 2025 revenue growth rate.

But I think the biggest concern is how fast management can bring down the Reinvestment rate to one given by the growth equation. In practice, this would mean having a more cost-effective acquisition program. This could come from paying less and/or extracting more synergies from the acquisitions.

In this context, I worry about the effectiveness of the acquisitions.

  • From 2017 to 2024, the company spent USD 2.5 billion on acquisitions.
  • During the period, PAT went from USD 111 million in 2017 to USD 291 million in 2025.
  • Assuming all the profits came from the acquisition, there was a USD 180 million growth in profits.
  • Assuming this figure annually, we have a 14-year payback period.

I know this is a rough calculation, but it does point to a long payback period. This implies some overpaying and/or not enough synergy. For a company whose growth is dependent on acquisitions, this raises a big red flag.

Conclusion

Over the past 8 years, Parsons has delivered top-line and bottom-line growth. But revenue growth was driven more by acquisitions rather than organic growth. Profit growth was due more to higher revenue and better fixed cost control rather than margin expansion.

At the same time, its overall financial performance remains below average compared to its peers. Return on capital continues to lag, and despite an improving EBIT margin trend, the company still ranks near the bottom in absolute margin levels. The unlevered free cash flow margin is similarly weak, reflecting high reinvestment demands.

In short, while Parsons has scale and growth momentum, its capital efficiency and profitability metrics suggest it still has ground to cover to match best-in-class performance in its sector.

You can understand why I would only rate Parsons as moderately sound from a fundamental perspective. At the same time, my valuation did not provide any margin of safety. This is not an investment from a long-term value investment perspective.

data.title: Stocks Take Another Tariff To The Shin
data.description:
Markets sagged again Friday after a fresh round of tariff threats landed like a sandbag, flattening retailers still wheezing from the last batch. Stocks clawed back from session lows, but still logged a 2% weekly drop. This trade war is the market’s Newtonian nemesis - what goes up meets tariffs and comes down.

Level Change 5/23/25 (%)

-0.6 Dow

-1.0 Nasdaq

-0.9 Nasdaq 100

-0.7 S&P 500

-0.2 S&P 400

-0.6 S&P 600

Trade returned to the front burner - and promptly boiled over. President Trump declared that talks with the EU are “going nowhere” and vowed a “straight 50% tariff” on all EU goods starting June 1. That’s more than twice the 20% bluff, quintuple the 10% goodwill pause, and roughly par for the posturing.

This followed reports that the US would reject the EU’s latest offer on a call with Trade Rep Jamieson Greer. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, reprising his role as tariff translator, said countries must “negotiate in good faith or face the US simply setting a tariff rate.” It’s trade policy by way of “clean your room, or I’ll do it for you.”

The EU didn’t flinch. Trade Commissioner Maroš Šefčovič insisted that “mutual respect, not threats,” should guide policy, and pledged to defend EU interests. Analysts pointed out the White House vow wasn’t an executive order (yet) and called it a classic Trump tactic: threaten big, negotiate later.

Apple (AAPL -3%) took its turn in the barrel after Trump warned that iPhones must be made in the US or face a 25% tariff - “at least.” Precision vagueness aside, Wedbush dismissed the idea as “a fairy tale.” Tripling the iPhone’s price just to stamp “Made in America” on the back might rupture even Cupertino’s famed reality-distortion field.

Of course, there’s a reason iPhones aren’t made in America. Apple spent billions training engineers abroad, where China and India offer deeper talent pools at a fraction of US wages. Steve Jobs put it bluntly to President Obama in 2010: Apple needed 30,000 industrial engineers. “You can’t find that many in America to hire,” he said. “If you could educate these engineers, we could move more manufacturing plants here.”

Fifteen years later, the gap remains, and Trump’s tariff-driven wish won’t close it overnight. Attention spans matter, especially when paired with a long-term plan. Just ask China.

Retailers didn’t wait for tariffs to wreck margins - they jumped first. Ross Stores (ROST -9.9%) tanked after yanking its full-year outlook, blaming “evolving trade policies” and stubborn inflation. Deckers Outdoor (DECK -19.9%) fell even harder, warning of weakened demand and tariff tolls on its Hoka and Ugg brands. When shoes drop this hard, they leave a crater.

The Fed hasn’t exactly broken into song, either.

Chicago’s Austan Goolsbee said the economy is holding up for now but warned that tariff-driven stagflation is “the central bank’s worst situation.” He’s still hopeful the US can return to the solid growth it enjoyed before the April 2 “Liberation Day” shock, and suggested interest rates might be “a fair bit below where they are today” in 10 to 16 months. With luck, that’s enough time for the baby parade to march out of earshot.

And that was Friday: fresh tariff threats, falling shoes, and a president confident that wishful thinking can outpace engineering shortages.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: GLD Vs. IAU: I Prefer IAU After U.S. Credit Rating Downgrade
data.description:
GLD, IAU, and U.S. credit rating

I last wrote on the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:GLD) on April 11. That article was titled "GLD Vs. SLV: Higher Recession Probability Makes GLD More Timely." As hinted in the title, the analysis is more oriented toward the near term. It rated GLD as a strong buy due to its role as hedging against the following recession risks at that time:

Recent changes in geopolitics have increased the probability of a recession. This leads me to see a better return/risk ratio for GLD than SLV. I expect GLD to demonstrate better resilience in market downturns both in terms of lower drawdowns and quicker recovery time. In contrast, SLV has more exposure to industrial applications, which tends to suffer negative impacts in the case of a recession.

As for the iShares Gold Trust ETF (NYSEARCA:IAU), I last wrote on it back in March in an article titled "GDX Vs. IAU: Why I Prefer IAU For Our Gold Exposure." That analysis is also geared toward the near term. It rated IAU as a buy because I saw it as a better way to capitalize on the gold price rally than gold miners.

Since those articles, there have been several changes in our macroeconomic parameters. The most consequential one to me was the recent downgrade of U.S. government credit ratings. In case you're unfamiliar with this development, the following news provides the background needed for the rest of this article. The downgrade immediately caused reaction in both the bonds and currency markets. Combined with other developments since my last writing (mostly the tariff drama), the dollar index (i.e., the ICE US Dollar Index, DXY) has declined substantially in the past month or so, as you can see from the following chart. The ICE US Dollar Index currently stands at 99.88, only slightly above the average level of 98.40 in the past 10 years.

On May 16, Moody’s Ratings downgraded the U.S. credit rating, citing an inability of the nation to address large and growing deficits. This downgrade means that for the first time ever, all three major credit ratings agencies have downgraded U.S. credit below their top rating. Moody’s warning comes as lawmakers are considering budget reconciliation legislation that would make America’s fiscal situation significantly worse.

These changes have promoted me to rethink my gold thesis for the longer term, and my thesis in this article are twofold. First, I expect the impacts for the U.S. credit downgrade to be long-lasting and to keep pressuring the dollar index. As gold is priced in dollars, this leads me to feel bullish about both IAU and GLD (and other similar gold ETFs). Second, for long-term holdings, I prefer IAU over GLD due to its lower fees. These considerations have led me to adjust my rating on GLD to buy and my rating on IAU to strong buy.

GLD and IAU ETFs: Basic facts

Before going into further details, let’s start with a quick intro for GLD and IAU in case there are readers new to them. Both funds are categorized as commodity funds as they're backed by physical gold bullion as detailed in their fund descriptions below.

GLD Description: GLD tracks the gold spot price, less expenses and liabilities, using gold bars held in London vaults. GLD is the first to market to invest directly in physical gold. Actively traded, the shares provide deep liquidity. NAV for the fund is determined using the LBMA PM Gold Price (formerly the London PM Gold Fix), so GLD has an extremely close relationship with spot prices.

IAU Description: IAU tracks the gold spot price, less expenses and liabilities, using gold bars held in vaults around the world. IAU is an efficient way for investors to hold physical gold. The fund removes the complexities for retail investors of buying, transporting, storing and insuring physical gold. Using a grantor trust structure, the fund is a very stable fund.

To my knowledge, GLD and IAU are two of the largest and oldest gold ETFs with GLD incepted in 2004 and IAU a year later in 2005. GLD manages a large AUM of $97.52 billion, and IAU's AUM is also sizable at $45.93 billion. A notable difference lies in their expense ratios, with GLD having a higher ratio of 0.40% compared to IAU's 0.25%, and I will further elaborate on this difference later.

In recent years, a few new competing ETFs have been issued that could offer more attractive alternatives. For example, GLDM has accumulated an AUM of $14.48 billion and charges an expense ratio of 0.10%, offering an attractive combination of liquidity and fees when compared to GLD and IAU. I will say more about these alternatives later in this article.

GLD and IAU: What history taught me

With the above introduction, now let me get back to my thesis and explain why I expect the U.S. credit downgrade to generate long-lasting impacts. To properly contextualize things, the chart below shows how rare such downgrades have been historically. Moody's has maintained its Aaa rating for the United States throughout the ups and downs in the past. I do see good reasons for Moody's downgrade at this junction, given the uncertainties of the budget reconciliation.

These uncertainties could cause a range of potential long-term impacts, including the loss of investor confidence, the reduced demand for U.S. assets, and also higher borrowing costs for the U.S. government. But the impact that I fear the most is the erosion of U.S. dollar’s global reserve status. While immediate and drastic changes are unlikely, a credit rating downgrade could weaken the dollar's status as the world's primary reserve currency over the long term. This could cause other countries to gradually diversify away from dollar-denominated reserves.

Both IAU and GLD could help investors hedge this long-term effect in at least two ways. First, if/when dollar’s status as a reserve currency weakens, I expect it to reduce global demand for the dollar, causing a sustained decline. As aforementioned, gold is priced in dollars (at least for the foreseeable future), a decline in dollar index tends to boost gold prices. Second, if/when other countries seek to diversify their reserve, I expect gold to be a top candidate in their diversification.

Of course, I do not expect these above considerations to play out in the near term – and I’ve warned you that this article is more oriented toward the long term. For a long-term holding, IAU’s lower fee compared to GLD could accumulate to a tangible difference. For example, the next chart shows the returns from GLD and IAU in the past 10 years with an initial balance of $10,000. As seen, in this example, IAU would end with a balance of $30,386, outperforming GLD's $29,940 by $446. For investors holding larger positions, the difference would scale proportionally.

Other risks and final thoughts

Investing in GLD and IAU entails a common set of risks such as counterparty risk, tracking errors, etc. Here, I will focus less on these generic risks and risks that are more pertinent to the analysis presented in this article.

I argued why the U.S. credit rating downgrade has made the return/risk ratio more attractive for GLD and IAU. However, investors need to bear in mind that the downgrade could have also made the return/risk ratio of other asset classes more attractive too. As a notable example, the downgrade has caused fluctuations of treasury rates too, as you can see from the following figure. The 10-Year Treasury Rate (I:10YTR) rose past 4.5% afterward the downgrade was announced and currently sits at 4.58%. Treasury rates with longer maturity rose even more with 30-year treasury rates now hovering above 5% as of this writing.

I also want to get back to the issue of fees. IAU's fee of 0.25% is lower relative to GLD’s 0.40%. However, it's still quite high relative to large equity and/or bonds funds (which are as low as 0.03% these days). Even among gold ETFS, more recent additions such GLDM and IAU now offer significantly reduced fees in the range of ~0.10%. We do have sizable positions in IAU for both our growth and withdrawal accounts, as you can see from the following updates we recently provided in our Investing Group (Envision Early Retirement). These are our legacy IAU shares that we bought years back. We're still holding on to them as the switch costs (mostly taxes) far outweigh the fee savings. As communicate with our members, for new additions, we will go with GLDM.

To close, recent changes in our macroeconomic conditions have promoted me to reexamine IAU and GLD with a long-term orientation. I believe the recent U.S. credit downgrade will have long-lasting impacts on the dollar index and subsequently gold pricing. As such, I feel bullish on both IAU and GLD, and specially suggest investors with a long-term timeframe to consider IAU (or other gold funds with lower fees if you don’t trade often).

data.title: Palantir: Expect Major Sentiment Upside From The Golden Dome
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR), the stock is up by 4% in price. In my portfolio, my Palantir position is up by 53.1% at the time of this writing. I am not adding to my position at this time due to short-term overextended sentiment.

Palantir is positioned to change the modern defense landscape as part of the team behind the ambitious U.S. Golden Dome missile defense program—a visionary plan to intercept advanced missile threats using artificial intelligence and space technology. In collaboration with elite partners SpaceX (SPACE) and Anduril, Palantir will utilize its elite platforms to aggregate and analyze important satellite data, enabling timely and accurate responses to emerging threats. This represents a significant moment where Silicon Valley is boldly challenging established defense contractors. At this juncture, investors are presented with a unique opportunity to participate early on in Palantir's ascension as the integral AI component of intelligent-tech global security.

Golden Dome for a Golden Age

Palantir will be the intelligence backbone for the U.S. government’s proposed Golden Dome missile defense system—a Trump-supported program designed to intercept missiles from hypersonics to ICBMs using artificial intelligence and space technology. Key partners of Palantir include SpaceX, which has been assigned the task of deploying a constellation of 400–1,000+ tracking satellites, and Anduril Industries, which will provide autonomous drones and next-generation sensor payloads. Palantir’s platforms will collect satellite sensor data, use artificial intelligence to identify threats, and direct interceptor systems. The defense network would be delivered by way of subscription instead of through procurement.

Palantir's association with the Golden Dome is evidence of the evolution of the current defense landscape—it represents the emergence of Silicon Valley challenging established defense contractors in critical national security projects. Such a significant contract awarded to the SpaceX–Palantir–Anduril trio would be a substantial victory for technology innovators, while signifying the importance of rapid-scaling AI systems to provide modern and competitive solutions. Palantir will be part of a new wave of AI-first defense succeeding legacy contractors and keeping the West firmly on the offensive in terms of technological superiority over adversaries.

Financials & Return Potential

The Golden Dome will provide Palantir with substantial financial growth potential. The project, potentially involving a contract of around $175 billion over the medium to long term, begins with a $25 billion contract currently being processed by Congress. Palantir is the prime software provider for the SpaceX-led bid, which could see massive amounts of revenue growth through its data integration, AI, and command and control solutions. If Palantir is awarded even a very low single digit percentage of the project (either by contract or annual subscription), that would be in excess of several billions of dollars of revenue over time. Therefore, the expected ~$3.9B revenue base in FY 2025 will see a significant boost.

The subscription-based model suggested in the Golden Dome partnership has the potential to shift Palantir's revenues from standard government contracts to subscription-based, high-margin, recurring-revenue deals. The Pentagon will rationally have concerns about enduring ongoing costs, but a subscription model provides higher service quality in the long run and ongoing development. Ultimately, a thriving U.S. macroeconomy (which is founded on the sustainable growth of the country’s own corporate leaders, like Palantir) is just as important to national security as defense budgets are.

Large government contracts, like the Golden Dome, can truly boost Palantir’s profitability. The company is now GAAP profitable with a 20% operating margin. These long-term, high-margin contracts will demand extensive upfront software development, and once the software is in production, the subscription revenue has minimal incremental cost, which increases margins over time. Also, Palantir has already shown success in the market handling large contracts such as its $1.3B U.S. Army Maven AI system, and management is clearly masterful at scaling revenue efficiently. The key point is that as software reuse becomes more prominent in the Golden Dome subscription model, Palantir's margin on the project can increase further, vastly offsetting their initial investments in R&D.

In all cases in the above table, Palantir's status as an integrator in long-term defense programs like the Golden Dome will add revenue stability that routinely would not be found in high-growth tech companies. Even in a bear-case scenario, having steady government contracts and renewals would provide added stability around innovation and investment in R&D. The bull case speaks to a more powerful upside potential, should Palantir achieve entrenched status as the AI-defense infrastructure for Western nations and its broader allies (India, Asia-Pacific allies—speculative at this stage); this is plausible given the international U.S.-led agenda of global interdependence the current administration is operating. However, it should still be considered that Palantir's current valuation (GAAP TTM P/E >500) has already built in a great deal of optimism, so the company will need to execute flawlessly to justify and sustain market sentiment.

Sentiment Volatility Drivers

Congressional approval on defense funding will be a significant driver of sentiment for Palantir's prospects. If Congress acts quickly to approve the proposed funding for the Golden Dome missile defense contract (the currently proposed "big beautiful bill" is $25B), then investor sentiment will likely improve. If, however, the budget is delayed, or cut back, it will increase concern on revenue growth not being as high as anticipated. As Palantir has recently outlined its FY2025 revenue guidance (+36%, largely due to strong demand for AI), investors will be looking for information in upcoming guidance related to any shifts in government spending.

Palantir's current valuation relies heavily on its ability to deliver on large projects. Achieving milestones in a timely manner would only enhance investor optimism for the stock. This includes prototyping a satellite-based "custody layer" (a dedicated network of satellites that maintain continuous tracking) of missile threats from launch to interception by 2026, in conjunction with Anduril and SpaceX. However, delays, overruns, technical issues, or missile-tracking reliability issues could quickly sour investor interest and rapidly compress Palantir’s valuation. Given the currently elevated state of the company's stock, any negative news related to something like a lost contract or feasibility concerns could have an outsized impact on the stock in the short term.

While Palantir is a defense contractor, investors are likely to associate it more broadly with the AI industry. Therefore, its stock price may increase or decrease based on investor sentiment regarding tech and AI. For example, while sustained investor enthusiasm for AI will support valuations for Palantir, any downturn (like the bursting of an "AI bubble" related to Big Tech funding cuts or slower AI-related capex scaling), could have a negative impact on the market's preferences for high multiple stocks, including Palantir. A high valuation places Palantir more at risk in these sentiment-moving events. However, you should seriously consider that greater volatility is the price of long-term excellence in portfolio returns. Dips in stable companies' valuations that have strong management and incredible technology moats is a reason to add to positions, not sell. Always invest with the macro thesis, in which Palantir is front-and-center of long-term global security amid international democratic alliance consolidation.

Long-Term Outlook

By 2030, should Palantir execute effectively, it could become as critical to the defense of the West as GPS or the internet (crucial, yet in Palantir's case, almost imperceptible). Military leaders may rely on Palantir dashboards to make real-time strategic decisions, as they currently depend on their own radar or satellite image analysis for such instances. Palantir software would coordinate multi-domain operations seamlessly that encompass not only cyber defense, but also satellite targeting and conventional military responses, in order to provide one unified operational plan that leverages bleeding-edge AI. In this role, Palantir would be irreplaceable, effectively establishing itself as a critical defense utility. Eventually, it will likely become a dividend powerhouse.

Economically, I’d liken Palantir's evolution to Salesforce's (CRM) evolution into enterprise Customer Relationship Management—I could imagine Palantir evolving into the "central command platform" for governments. Except, I think Palantir's management will make the company's moat and market position much longer-lasting than Salesforce's, not least because of the high-barrier defense relationships and highly sensitive commercial-sector network effects. By 2030, I estimate Palantir's only real competitors would come from a disruptive change in intelligent technology itself (another fundamental technological revolution—unlikely in our lifetimes, though with AI nothing is off the table).

Palantir's long-term focus, supported by ultra-aware founders Alex Karp and Peter Thiel, supports the buy-and-hold bull case.

Portfolio Positioning

Seeking Alpha investors with a horizon of more than five years should consider Palantir as a core holding in their "Next Generation Defense and AI" investment strategy. Palantir is uniquely well-positioned, using its first-mover advantage in expanding markets. The company is generating momentum from its recent major wins (Golden Dome, NATO, and major commercial contracts). Palantir is the only company that I am aware of that combines a specialized approach to defense, heavy investment in bleeding-edge AI, and is of a larger scale in this cross-market above cloud providers, traditional defense primes, or smaller AI businesses. This suggests that Palantir is well-situated for sustained long-term growth.

Moreover, I think investors should be aggressively optimistic in weighting Palantir in portfolios, while sizing positions carefully due to the fact Palantir is more volatile than a larger stable mega-cap position (perhaps about half, or even ⅔, of capital compared to a mega-cap position is wise).

Palantir shares may remain volatile for a while as the valuation is still up for debate. However, more sophisticated investors should put greater emphasis on core metrics related to Palantir's government deal pipeline, commercial customer additions, net dollar retention, and marginal improvements. If you see a growing deal backlog, growing customer base (with the Fortune 500 companies like we see reported recently), and improving operating margins, then you should be supportive of Palantir's strategic narrative and associated valuation in the interim. However, if government revenue begins to slow down, I will be looking for signs that traction is slowing more generally (i.e., by tracking international expansion in customer base, growth in U.S. government contracts, etc.). Currently, I do think there are favorable trends that indicate Palantir's growth narrative remains fully intact for the indefinite future. I find the downside risks in terms of pure operational traction to be negligible.

Conclusion: Buy

Palantir’s engagement with the Golden Dome project showcases the company as a once-in-a-generation defense contractor, in an era where software will be as critical as hardware. The combination of the company’s extensive ties to national security with the advent of a scalable subscription-based model is distinct. Given Palantir stock’s current price, there is little room for error in execution, but investment gains could be substantial if management keeps the organization on a hyper-successful track through 2030. For patient investors willing to accept both volatility and political sensitivity, Palantir is an attractive, asymmetric bet with the potential for outsized returns as AI becomes ever more pivotal in a world where intelligence-driven, software-defined advantages will shape global dynamics.

data.title: DVYE: A Strategic Bet On Inflation, Commodities, And Global Power Shifts
data.description:
A Rare Value in a Market Priced for Perfection

Emerging markets are a cyclical asset class, and history shows that they don’t stay out of favor forever. From 2001 through 2010, emerging market equities outperformed the S&P500 by a wide margin, driven by a weak dollar, China’s infrastructure boom, and soaring demand for commodities. But since 2011, emerging markets have underperformed dramatically, weighed down by dollar strength, political instability, and the meteoric rise of U.S. tech. This 13-year stretch of underperformance has left many investors disillusioned - but may also be setting up one of the most compelling contrarian opportunities in global markets.

Today, U.S. equities are priced for perfection. The S&P500 trades at a forward P/E of over 22, fueled by investor enthusiasm around artificial intelligence and a handful of mega-cap names. In contrast, the iShares Emerging Markets Dividend ETF (NYSE:DVYE) trades at a forward P/E of under 9x and a price-to-book near 1.0x, valuation levels not seen since the early 2000s. At the same time, DVYE offers a dividend yield of around 11%, delivering income now, not speculative growth later.

As macro conditions shift toward persistent inflation, global de-dollarization and supply chain realignment, emerging markets may be poised to outperform. DoubleLine Capital CEO and "Bond King" Jeffrey Gundlach recently warned that U.S. stocks are “overextended,” and that the next cycle will favor value and international assets. Many investors already started to sell U.S. equities, directing allocations to underpriced segments abroad.

This sets the stage for a potential long-term rotation, and DVYE offers a rare opportunity to capitalize, with cash flow, valuation, and macro alignment all working in its favor.

Fund overview

DVYE offers exposure to high-yielding equities in emerging markets, tracking the Dow Jones Emerging Markets Select Dividend Index. This passively managed index screens for strong dividend companies across emerging markets, selecting stocks based on annual dividend yield while also applying filters for dividend sustainability, liquidity, and payout ratios. Companies with unsustainable dividends are excluded from the index.

Rather than weighting its constituents by market capitalization, DVYE weights them based on the size of their dividend payments. This methodology results in a distinct portfolio of high-yielding companies across a broad array of value-oriented sectors. Other traditional emerging market ETFs differ significantly from DVYE in the sense that they often lean heavily toward tech or growth-oriented names.

DVYE prioritizes income generation in value-heavy sectors and economies where commodities and infrastructure are key economic drivers, offering a differentiated way to play the emerging markets.

Country Allocation

DVYE has significant exposure to Latin America and Asia, with its top country allocations consisting of:

This heavy tilt toward Brazil and China underscores DVYE’s orientation toward resource-rich economies and Asian markets. Smaller allocations to countries such as Thailand, Mexico, and UAE serve to add further diversification and strategic yield generation.

DVYE is heavily weighted toward value-oriented, cyclical sectors:

This sector tilt aligns with DVYE’s focus on value and income strategy, favoring companies in capital-intensive industries.

Expense Ratio

DVYE carries an expense ratio of 0.49%, which is reasonable in comparison to other funds which focus on emerging markets and dividend yield. In short, the fee gives investors efficient access to a portfolio of high-yield emerging market stocks that would be tough to replicate individually.

DVYE’s holdings include a blend of state-owned enterprises, financial institutions, and commodity-related firms. Notable names include:

These companies are dominant players in their respective sectors and often have strong government backing.

Fund Performance

DVYE’s performance has lagged the S&P500 over the past decade. This reflects the broader underperformance of emerging markets rather than the fund’s construction. While the S&P500 has been fueled by strong growth in large-cap technology names, DVYE’s focus on commodity-driven and cyclical sectors in developing economies has led to greater volatility and extended periods of relative underperformance.

DVYE’s total return profile, however, looks more favorable when income is factored in. With a trailing 12-month dividend yield near 11%, the fund has consistently delivered strong cash flows in all market environments. During periods of inflation or rising commodity prices, such as 2021, DVYE has shown the ability to outperform broad equity benchmarks. While it may not match the growth of the S&P500 in tech-led bull markets, DVYE offers meaningful diversification and strong upside in environments that favor value, income, and global assets.

DVYE has shown more volatility than the S&P500, outperforming in commodity-driven years like 2016 but underperforming during broader market selloffs. In comparison to SPY, DVYE's returns tend to be more cyclical and macro-sensitive.

It is important for prospective investors to remember that past performance is not indicative of future results. DVYE’s returns are highly sensitive to macroeconomic shifts and currency fluctuations, which can significantly impact performance in both directions. For this reason, investors should consider the role of exchange rates, local market risk, and dividend sustainability when determining whether they want to allocate to DVYE.

A Bet on Inflation: Yield, Scarcity, and the Structural Setup

In a world where inflation continues to linger and policymakers are cornered by rising debt loads, investors need assets that align with this new macro regime. DVYE fits that bill.

Unlike U.S. large-cap indices, DVYE offers a real yield that outpaces inflation. With a trailing 12-month dividend yield of approximately 11%, it dramatically exceeds the payouts of developed market ETFs like SPY (1.3%), VYM (3.1%), or HDV (3.6%). DVYE's investment thesis isn't predicated on hypothetical appreciation but rather real operating incomes. In an inflationary environment, cash flow today is worth far more than speculative growth tomorrow.

DVYE’s sector allocation is built for cycles marked by prolonged periods of inflation and stagflation. Overweights in energy, financials, and materials position the fund to benefit from rising input costs, global commodity demand, and persistent monetary debasement. These sectors have historically outperformed during inflationary and rate-hiking regimes, while long-duration growth assets tend to underperform due to their reliance on low discount rates and future earnings.

Down About Debt

Across the developed and developing world, sovereign debt is surging. In the U.S. alone, annual interest expense is projected to exceed $1 trillion by 2026. Globally, central banks face a painful choice between hiking rates to fight inflation or printing to finance debt, either of which accelerates inflation. This dynamic creates a pernicious feedback loop: more debt, more interest payments, and more money creation. Many central banks also chose to keep rates steady as markets expect rate cuts, creating potential for market swings as disappointment sets in. DVYE benefits from this structurally inflationary backdrop by delivering immediate cash flows through high dividend yields to offset inflation, offering investors tangible returns in a world increasingly defined by monetary distortion.

"You can't permanently run a large current account deficit without having a currency crisis." - Warren Buffett, Fortune interview, 2003

On May 16, 2025, Moody’s Ratings downgraded the U.S. credit outlook, citing the federal government’s inability to rein in deficits. With this move, all three major credit rating agencies have now placed U.S. sovereign debt below their top rating (downgrading from AAA to AA+), a historic precedent. While markets largely brushed off the downgrade, wise investors have taken note.

Mounting fiscal imbalances pose long-term risks to both the dollar and U.S. debt markets. Many emerging market countries hold significant amounts of U.S. debt, and a weakening dollar improves their debt-servicing capacity, potentially unlocking growth. In a weak dollar environment, exposure to non-dollar-denominated assets becomes a driver of outperformance: gains in local currencies translate into higher returns for dollar-based investors. DVYE stands to benefit from this rebalancing, offering targeted exposure to non-dollar economies.

Moreover, DVYE and other high-dividend equity strategy funds are better postured for inflation than large-cap U.S. ETFs like the SPY because their profits are immediate, tangible, and often backed by hard assets. Many of the companies in DVYE’s portfolio operate in sectors with real pricing power, whether it's energy, utilities, or basic materials, allowing them to pass on rising costs rather than absorb them.

Tech Can Be Tricky

While traditional economic thinking often frames technological advancement as inherently disinflationary, the reality is more complex. Advances in machine learning and AI have allowed companies to optimize pricing with surgical precision, constantly testing consumer willingness to pay' and adjusting prices in real time. This doesn’t just preserve pricing power: it institutionalizes it. Even as input costs decline, algorithms are designed to maximize margins, not pass savings to consumers. The result isn’t a deflationary tech utopia, but a landscape where data-driven pricing reinforces profitability, often at the expense of the consumer.

In the current inflationary landscape, marked by rising input costs and commodity scarcity, DVYE stands out by providing exposure to resource-driven sectors and companies that thrive under these conditions: energy, materials, and financials. This exposure comes through a diversified, income-generating portfolio grounded in the physical economy rather than financial engineering.

Geopolitical Diversification That Matters

Currencies Take Center Stage

DVYE is not just diversified across sectors; it’s diversified across political systems, currencies, and natural resources. Nearly 60% of the fund is allocated to Brazil, China, Indonesia, India, and South Africa. These nations are driving commodity exports, infrastructure development, and trade outside of Western frameworks.

This exposure introduces currency risk, as DVYE does not hedge foreign exchange fluctuations. While this adds volatility, it also opens the door to diversification benefits, especially in an environment where the U.S. dollar may be peaking in relative strength. Rapid shifts in global monetary policy, including rising interest rates and active de-dollarization efforts, have made currency movements more impactful for investors with emerging market exposure. Gains (or losses) in the underlying local currencies can materially impact DVYE’s total return in U.S. dollar terms.

The monetary policy landscape within DVYE’s top exposures is far from uniform. Brazil, which represents the fund’s largest country weight, aggressively raised interest rates to over 14%, among the highest in the world, to fight inflation. This tightening cycle has supported the Brazilian real (BRL), which in turn helps preserve the U.S. dollar value of dividends paid by Brazilian companies. In contrast, countries like China, Indonesia, and Poland have pursued more accommodative monetary policy, leading to weaker local currencies.

For U.S. investors, these currency movements have direct implications for dividend income. Since DVYE distributes dividends in U.S. dollars, any appreciation of a local currency versus the dollar can increase the translated value of payouts, even if the local dividend remains unchanged. Conversely, if a currency like the Chinese yuan or Indonesian rupiah weakens, then dividends paid in local terms may shrink when converted to dollars, reducing the effective income investors receive. In other words, even stable or growing dividends at the company level may not translate into stable income for U.S. holders if the foreign currency is depreciating. This makes DVYE’s yield not just a function of company performance, but also of monetary divergence and currency strength - two macro forces that U.S. investors must factor in when evaluating both income potential and total return.

Global reserve currencies are fulcrum drivers of domestic economies. If a country is privileged enough to have its currency take on global reserve status, it benefits from:

1. Outsized global demand for their currency (lower borrowing costs)

2. Control over global liquidity

3. Geopolitical leverage

4. Reinforcing network effects

Historically speaking global reserve currencies tend to last between 80 to 110 years on average:

“The world is entering an era where the U.S. dollar's role as the dominant reserve currency is being increasingly questioned. The shift benefits countries with real assets—especially commodity-rich emerging markets.”— Ray Dalio, LinkedIn post, October 2022

While it is impossible to predict how long the dollar will remain the global reserve currency, a visual examination of the DXY’s trade-weighted strength suggests it may be reaching a relative top:

Trading Trends

Furthermore, DVYE serves as a strategic counterweight in an era when capital flows are increasingly shaped by high-frequency trading (HFT), advanced machine learning models, and Monte Carlo-based optimizations. Investors gravitate toward U.S. large-cap indices like the S&P 500, believing past outperformance will continue, but these assumptions are now deeply embedded in the algorithms and models that dominate modern portfolio construction. The systems driving price discovery already "know" which markets have historically delivered the best returns, and that collective knowledge is priced in. What appears to be a safe or efficient bet may actually reflect consensus positioning and diminishing forward potential. In contrast, DVYE offers exposure to markets where price movements are still largely driven by macro fundamentals, commodity cycles, and trade flows, making it a valuable diversifier in an increasingly model-saturated investment landscape.

In addition to diversifying against model-driven efficiencies, DVYE also provides strategic exposure to non-dollar economies, giving investors access to a broader set of currencies, political systems, and growth drivers. While DVYE’s unhedged currency exposure introduces added volatility, it also enhances the fund’s diversification benefits, particularly if the U.S. dollar begins a longer-term decline. For investors willing to embrace this risk, DVYE offers a compelling way to participate in the structural evolution of the global economy.

Strategic Commodity Exposure Without Roll Yield Risks

Unlike developed economies like the U.S., many emerging markets are predicated on resource extraction and export, with commodities forming the backbone of GDP and government revenues. DVYE, through its holdings in global producers like Petrobras, Vale, and Vedanta, serves as a commodity proxy.

Most investors today have little or no exposure to commodities, despite their role as one of the best-performing asset classes in inflationary environments. And frankly, for good reason. Direct commodity investing via futures is complex, often inefficient, and rarely intuitive for retail investors.

The primary issue is roll yield. Because commodity futures expire, ETFs that track them must constantly sell expiring contracts and buy new ones. When the market is in contango (where an asset's future price is higher than it's current spot proce) - as it often is with oil, natural gas, and agriculture - this “rolling” creates a negative yield that erodes returns over time. Even if spot prices rise, futures-based ETFs can lose money due to this structural drag. Add to that volatility, leverage decay, and lack of income - and commodities become a tough standalone asset class for long-term portfolios.

DVYE solves this. It gives investors indirect, strategic exposure to commodities by owning dividend-paying companies in commodity-rich emerging markets - like Petrobras (PBR), Vale (VALE), and Vedanta (VEDL). These companies benefit from rising prices without the structural flaws of futures investing. And they pay you to wait, with yields that average 5–10%+ at the company level.

“Money is made by discounting the obvious and betting on the unexpected.”— George Soros, The Alchemy of Finance, 1987

Historically, commodities and the U.S. dollar have an inverse relationship, with strong dollar cycles coinciding with suppressed commodity valuations. When commodities are cheap and ignored, they tend to deliver their best returns. The chart above shows how rare - and potentially powerful - today's setup is; commodities are near their lowest valuation relative to equities in over 50 years. At the same time, the U.S. dollar remains near historic highs. This divergence sets the stage for a potential macro reversal.

In short, DVYE offers a smarter, more sustainable way to get inflation-sensitive, commodity-aligned exposure without the complexities of direct commodity trading. With commodities still historically undervalued relative to equities and bonds, DVYE presents a unique opportunity to position ahead of a potential rotation.

A Bet on Strategic Global Supply Chains

In the next decade, the success of national economies will be defined not just by tech innovation, but by control over logistics, trade routes, and supply chains. The world is fragmenting into blocs, and the countries that secure reliable access to energy, materials, and shipping lanes will lead. Technology can disrupt many things but it cannot rewrite geography.

DVYE offers investors exposure to countries and companies that sit at the heart of this global infrastructure realignment.

Consider the Strait of Malacca, one of the most important maritime chokepoints on the planet. Roughly 30% of global crude oil and 25% of total global trade pass through this narrow strait connecting the Indian Ocean to the South China Sea. DVYE has nearly 10% exposure to Indonesia, a nation with a front-row seat to the evolution of Asian maritime logistics. Holdings like Bank Rakyat Indonesia support infrastructure lending and trade finance for key projects around the Strait, projects increasingly supported by Chinese capital through Belt and Road partnerships.

That brings us to the Belt and Road Initiative (BRI) - China’s multi-decade global development strategy aimed at reshaping trade routes and economic influence across Asia, Africa, and Latin America. BRI is building ports, pipelines, railroads, and energy plants across more than 150 countries. The goal is clear: reduce dependence on U.S.-controlled sea lanes and financial systems, and build a resilient Eurasian trade bloc.

China has partnered heavily with economically vulnerable nations, such as Pakistan, Sri Lanka, and Kenya, to finance these projects. Critics argue that these partnerships saddle poor nations with unsustainable debt. But other economists view it as a brilliant geopolitical strategy: many of these resource-rich countries sit on strategic coastlines or maritime chokepoints, and under some BRI contracts, China retains rights to infrastructure assets (like ports) if the loans can’t be repaid. In this way, China could gain de facto control over key nodes in global supply chains, even beyond its borders.

DVYE holds companies that directly benefit from this effort: Petrobras and Vale (Brazil) are shipping energy and metals into Asia’s industrial expansion. ICBC and China Construction Bank (China) are financing ports, power plants, and high-speed rail lines across Africa and Southeast Asia. Vedanta (India) supplies the raw materials essential for development. Indonesian banks and logistics firms are enabling the buildout of regional trade capacity.

These companies and countries aren't just playing catch-up: they’re shaping the trade corridors of the next century. DVYE gives investors exposure to the real-world infrastructure behind economic growth — the logistics, ports, pipelines, and power lines that define whether a nation can export, manufacture, and grow in an increasingly multipolar world.

This supply chain thesis isn't easily disrupted. AI can simulate logistics but it can’t replace a port. A neural network can optimize inventory but it can’t build a railroad. DVYE gives you exposure to the countries actually laying that groundwork. In a global economy increasingly defined by trade friction, geography, and resource competition, these are the foundations of national advantage.

A Front-Row Seat to BRICS and De-Dollarization

The BRICS bloc, Brazil, Russia, India, China, and South Africa, now represents over 40% of the global population and more than 30% of global GDP (on a purchasing power parity basis). This group is increasingly united by a shared mission: to reduce dependence on the U.S. dollar and Western-led financial institutions. Trade is increasingly being settled in local currencies. Bilateral deals in yuan, rupees, and rubles are growing. Central banks across BRICS countries are stockpiling gold and other hard assets, while reducing their holdings of U.S. Treasuries.

If the global economy continues moving toward a multipolar structure, the dollar's reserve status will face pressure. A decline in dollar dominance would carry broad implications, potentially weakening U.S. capital markets and reducing the global demand for Treasuries. In this scenario, traditional U.S. equity exposure could be vulnerable.

Furthermore, DVYE offers direct exposure to the countries and companies benefiting from this geopolitical shift. Its holdings include state-backed banks, commodity producers, and industrial exporters that are central to the BRICS economic strategy. While not a perfect hedge, DVYE is less tethered to the U.S. dollar cycles than traditional U.S. equity funds - and many of its underlying companies stand to benefit from a weakening dollar, especially those aligned with BRICs trade.

In short, if de-dollarization keeps gaining ground, and BRICS nations stay aligned around trade, gold, and supply chain control, DVYE might just be one of the simplest ways to ride that shift. Especially for investors prioritizing yield and global diversification, it’s worth a closer look. It’s not just a bet on emerging markets. It’s a bet on a world rebalancing away from the dollar, and toward a more fragmented but resource-centric future.

Risks to Consider

While DVYE offers compelling yield and macro alignment, investors should be aware of several risks:

1. Geopolitical and Currency Volatility: Emerging markets are inherently more volatile than developed markets. Political instability, currency devaluation, or regulatory shifts can quickly impact local equity performance and dividend reliability. DVYE’s country exposure includes China, Brazil, and South Africa - all of which face unique sovereign and policy risks.

2. Dividend Variability: DVYE is not a fixed-income product - its distributions depend on the earnings and payout policies of its underlying companies. In times of economic stress, even historically reliable dividend payers can reduce or suspend payouts, impacting total return.

3. Limited Tech Exposure: DVYE is structurally underweight technology and long-duration growth stocks, which have driven much of the U.S. market’s performance in recent years. If AI, robotics, or digital infrastructure lead the next cycle, DVYE may lag more tech-heavy benchmarks.

4. De-Dollarization May Take Time: While de-dollarization and BRICS realignment are powerful macro themes, they are slow-moving and subject to setbacks. A resurgence in U.S. dollar strength or stabilization in U.S. rates could delay capital flows into emerging markets.

5. Sanctions and Index Rules: DVYE may hold companies in jurisdictions subject to international sanctions (i.e., Russia). Index providers sometimes rebalance or remove such holdings, which can impact performance or change country weighting unexpectedly.

Conclusion: A Unique Opportunity in a Shifting World

The iShares Emerging Markets Dividend ETF (DVYE) stands at the intersection of multiple structural trends: inflation resilience, commodity strength, global supply chain realignment, and geopolitical rebalancing. With a forward P/E under 9, a dividend yield near 11%, and exposure to economies increasingly shaping global trade and finance, DVYE offers investors both value and income in a world dominated by expensive, momentum-driven U.S. equities.

Unlike traditional emerging market ETFs that focus on growth, DVYE is income-oriented, sector-diversified, and positioned to benefit from long-term shifts in macroeconomic and geopolitical dynamics. It captures the rise of BRICS, the push for de-dollarization, and the strategic importance of real assets and trade infrastructure - all while avoiding the complexity of commodity futures and delivering consistent cash flow.

While DVYE carries inherent risks - such as currency fluctuations, geopolitical volatility, and dividend variability - these are the same forces that could amplify its upside; in a rapidly changing global order, volatility often cuts both ways. In a market where perfection is priced in and future returns are uncertain, DVYE provides exposure to underpriced, real-world businesses that are powering the next global economic cycle. For investors seeking diversification, yield, and contrarian-driven value, DVYE presents a compelling case as part of a broader allocation strategy.

data.title: UTF: Collect Dividends From The Growth Of Infrastructure
data.description:
Overview

Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (NYSE:UTF) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns from a diverse portfolio of assets that are aligned with infrastructure. In my opinion, investing into an area like infrastructure will always be important because these are the things that are essential to improving the quality of our life. This includes things like roads, pipelines, cell towers, and other utilities that we use on a daily basis without a second thought. I previously covered UTF and issued a buy rating due to the outlook of interest rate cuts at the time. However, the outlook on the macro-environment has shifted since then so I wanted to revisit UTF to provide some updated analysis.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that UTF's price has increased by about 8.7%. The fund rapidly recovered from the market decline experienced in reaction to President Trump's tariff proposals. When including all distributions that were paid out over the same time frame, the total return jumps up to 17.3%. One of the main appeals of maintaining exposure to a fund like UTF is the generous starting dividend yield of 7.2% and its monthly distributions. This makes UTF an enticing choice for investors seeking a source of reliable long-term income generation.

UTF has a solid history of performance but the latest annual report does reveal some weakness. The fund has demonstrated that it can efficiently generate consistent levels of income, but a bulk of the investments are still reliant on positive market conditions to fuel growth. The higher interest rate environment can be a challenge going forward and has the potential to slow UTF's growth. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy and investment methodology that UTF implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the fact sheet, UTF has managed assets of $3.3B that is spread across 259 different holdings. The fund provides exposure to companies that support the physical framework for modern societies to function. UTF is unique because it also provides exposure to companies outside of the US, which makes it a solid option for investors that want to diversify their exposure on a global scale. The breakdown of assets by region are as follows:

  • United States: 57%
  • Canada: 11%
  • United Kingdom: 5%
  • Australia: 4%
  • Other: 23% - (includes France, Japan, Mexico, Spain, Brazil, Hong Kong, India, Netherlands, China, Switzerland, New Zealand, Italy, Thailand, Luxembourg, Malaysia, Philippines, Greece, Sweden, Germany, Finland, Bermuda)

Looking at the sector breakdown, we can see that Electric investments make up the largest weight of the fund at 34%. This is followed by exposure to Midstream and Gas Distribution companies, making up 11% and 8% of assets respectively. Another 15% of assets are allocated to Corporate Bonds, which is UTF's way to strategically blend in some income-focused assets that can provide a baseline of net investment income through changing market conditions.

Despite UTF having 259 different positions within, the top ten holdings make up more than a third of total assets. Therefore, I would suggest that investors to do a bit of research on each of the underlying holdings just to get an understanding of each asset within. Since these top ten holdings have a large weight, we can assume that these will have the greatest impact on UTF's daily price changes and total return potential. NiSource (NI) is currently the largest holding at 4.2%. This is followed by varying weights to other large-cap names like NextEra Energy (NEE), Duke Energy (DUK), TC Energy (TRP), and American Tower (AMT). Here are some notable traits of some of the top holdings:

  • Enbridge (ENB): Recently announced $3B in annual utility investments.
  • The Southern Company (SO): Increased dividends for 23 consecutive years without disruption.
  • National Grid (NGG): Expects a compounded annual growth rate in earnings per share between 6% to 8%.

Lastly, UTF utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify its returns when an attractive opportunity presents itself. Leverage is currently equivalent to 28.52% of assets, which I consider to be moderate in today's high interest rate climate. Investors must consider the risk that leverage can also amplify losses if allocated towards an investment that doesn't go as planned. However, the fund has demonstrated that its strategy has been effective since inception. The price has increased by 27.8% while the total return is over 616% since inception.

Updated Valuation

Since UTF operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. As a result, UTF currently trades at a slight premium to NAV of about 3.1%. For reference, UTF has traded at an average discount to NAV of 0.5% over the last three year period. So while UTF may no longer be at an attractive discount level, we can see that the valuation remains in-line with peer funds below. Looking back over the last ten years, we can see that the discount window has gradually become smaller over time, which could be an indication that the fund is improving performance and efficiently growing its assets.

To get better clarity on the fund's internals, I reviewed the most recent annual report for 2024. Net investment income amounted to $0.53 per share while net realized gains landed at $2.31 per share. Looking back at prior years, we can see that net investment income levels have remained fairly consistent within the same range. This indicates that UTF has established a steady baseline of income collection from its investments and even though the net investment income levels aren't growing, the underlying investments are reliable through different market conditions.

However, net investment income aren't large enough to fully support the distributions on their own. Therefore, UTF is reliant on positive market conditions to fuel net realized gains. Historically performance indicates that UTF still suffers losses during market declines, as we can see from 2022's net realized loss of $2.56 per share. This reliance on net realized gains has ultimately slowed the NAV growth that can be experienced since UTF needs bull markets to grow. Just for reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $24.62 per share
  • 2021: $28.28 per share
  • 2022: $24.36 per share
  • 2023: $22.88 per share
  • 2024: $23.86 per share

A way the fund can improve its performance is to grow the levels of net investment income received. This would take away from the fund's reliance on net realized gains and provide UTF with much better price stability and resilience through market declines. Unfortunately, the industry of infrastructure seems to be sensitive to changes in the interest rate environment.

Risk

Infrastructure generally relies on a lot of debt financing as a way to fuel operations. Affordable debt can be used to fund the development of pipelines or new construction developments. The current elevated interest rate environment has made it a bit difficult for many infrastructure related businesses to be able to expand operations as planned. As a result, there can be a growing number of projects that are being put on the backlog until it becomes more cost efficient to pursue these projects.

The federal funds rate still remains near its ten year high, despite a few rate cuts taking place during Q4 of 2024. A higher interest rate is directly associated with a higher cost of capital. Looking at the chart below, we can see how UTF's price has shared a bit of an inverse relationship with interest rates. UTF's price increased throughout 2020 and 2021 when interest rates were extremely low. Conversely, UTF's price retracted down from its highs during 2022 and 2023 when interest rates were being increased.

Therefore, we can conclude that future interest rate cuts can serve as a catalyst for growth. Unfortunately, it doesn't seem like there is much confidence that sizeable rate cuts will actually happen over the remainder of the year. The Fed kept rates unchanged during their latest meeting as they continue to monitor the macro-economic environment. For instance, the ripple effects of President Trump's tariffs are still to be determined. I anticipate that a higher cost of goods will ultimately lead to higher inflation and will delay interest rate cuts. There might even be a chance that we see interest rates get increased if inflation rises.

Data suggests that the total amount of capital needed for infrastructure investments are already behind the demand. Across different sub-sectors like transportation, energy, and water, the anticipated investments are still not able to meet the demand for these services. A slowing rate of investments can ultimately suppress valuations across the sector and negatively impact UTF's growth over time.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.155 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.2%. If we multiply this monthly distribution by twelve, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $1.86 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, UTF has a mixed history of actually being able to support the distribution amounts with its earnings. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: ($1.25) per share
  • 2021: $5.51 per share
  • 2022: ($2.06) per share
  • 2023: $0.38 per share
  • 2024: $2.84 per share

As you can see, UTF has only out earned its distribution amount in two of the last five years. Now thankfully UTF had a very strong 2021 so the earnings were able to make up for both the losses experienced in 2022 and underwhelming performance in 2023. 2024's performance seems to have generated a sufficient amount of income to pay out the distributions. So while I don't think this performance is bad enough to warrant any distribution cuts, the overall sustainability of the distribution is something that I remain cautious about during market declines.

It's also worth noting that the distributions are typically funded by a combination of net investment income and net realized gains. These distributions are generally classified as ordinary dividend income, which could have the heaviest tax burdens associated. So if you want to maintain a position in UTF, it would be best utilized within a tax advantaged account to offset any potential tax burdens.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on UTF due to the fund's ability to generate income from the infrastructure exposure. By maintaining exposure to UTF, investors can capitalize on the essential services that improve the quality of our lives. There is always a growing demand for investment in infrastructure and although the current macro-environment may not be favorable, I believe that a long-term stance can reward patient investors. Future interest rate cuts can serve as a catalyst for growth as it increases the amount of investment capital that can flow into the infrastructure sectors. Despite the mixed performance in recent years, I anticipate that the distribution will remain consistent.

data.title: You Mess With The Don, You Get The Tariffs
data.description:
Introduction

The U.S. tariff situation has been in flux since "Liberation Day," April 2nd. The Trump administration has promised "90 deals in 90 days," with a deadline of July 2nd for countries across the planet to negotiate new deals with the United States, aimed primarily at lowering trade deficits.

Traditionally, in negotiations, we consider two kinds of incentives: carrots and sticks. The carrot is the reward, which in recent tariff negotiations has been missing. According to President Trump's messaging, the carrot is the ability to do trade with the U.S., something the world has effectively taken for granted in the last forty years. This means that there isn't much in the way to offer trading partners in the way of rewards, and so much of the negotiations have revolved around the stick, the punishment, which in this case, is tariffs.

The U.S. now has the highest effective tariff rate on imports in the last century, and it has become incredibly divisive among political circles regarding their effectiveness, economic impacts, and usefulness as a negotiation tool (read as: stick).

If you're finding it difficult to track what tariffs have been threatened, imposed, and suspended (don't worry, I am too), this tracker from Bloomberg has been a great aid.

We're at least 1/90 down, after the U.S. inked a framework for a trade deal with the UK back on May 8th. As a commenter in that article so aptly pointed out, this was a fairly surefire win for the administration, and was akin to "taking your cousin to prom." After all, we've had a "Special Relationship" with the UK for over a century that has guided cooperation between the two nations, and the UK has been pining for a new trade deal with the U.S. ever since its Brexit in 2020.

Seems we're over the time they razed parts of DC, including the White House, even if I'm personally not.

Kidding aside, this deal seemed to be a win-win for the U.S.-UK all but for a few line items, like the British not removing the Digital Services Tax on U.S. businesses, which resulted in the U.S. keeping the 10% baseline tariff rate on British goods minus some exclusions like steel and autos (up to a point).

Negotiations with the Chinese have been slow-going, with the 145% embargo-like tariff placed on Chinese imports, designed to bring China "to the negotiating table," having resulted in another 90-day pause, and a reduction to 30%. For the record, it's not far off the original 34% promised back on Liberation Day. On this point, we're still seeing reduced shipping volumes on fears from businesses that the situation won't stabilize soon.

Both the U.S. and Chinese markets have rebounded since the lows caused by the Liberation Day announcement, indicating positivity from market participants that a deal will indeed be reached soon.

Here are the two markets YTD returns:

Secretary Bessent: "Coming Soon"

To those who are persistent readers of executive office news (regardless of the executive), promises of results being "just around the corner" are nothing new.

Welcome to politics.
