On Friday the 23rd (today as of time of writing), Treasury Sec. Scott Bessent made statements that were along these lines, providing the usual bluster we see from politics: no specifics.

He said that there could be "several large trade deals" announced in the next couple of weeks, which includes the potential for movement on the deal with China, the most pressing.

The actual quote doesn't inspire too much confidence for me:

My sense is, over the next couple of weeks we’re going to have several large deals announced...[Regarding China], I expect that we will be negotiating in-person with them again.

...

These deals are moving quickly, and I think as we approach the end of the 90-day period, we’re going to see more and more of them announced...Many of the Asian countries have come with very good deals.

Bessent, when pressed on which countries we were negotiating with, declined to comment. He did say, "...we're far along with India."

Through all of this talk, the dollar sold off on fears that deals are not moving along as promised, due to the lack of details.

Trump: "A Little Problem"

Also on Friday the 23rd (today as of time of writing), President Trump took to Truth Social (DJT), his social media platform, to disparage both the European Union ("EU") and Apple Inc. (AAPL), threatening them both with tariffs.

EU Gets The Stick

Initially on Liberation Day, the EU was threatened with 20% tariffs, which commentators believed would do significant harm to both U.S. and EU economically.

However, on Friday, President Trump upped the ante, pushing for 50% tariffs on all goods from the EU, citing a litany of reasons in his post.

This may not be able to push the EU any faster, as their process relies on unanimity among its member states, which are politically divided and often slow to move. While the EU has a central government in Brussels, it is political neutered compared to fully federalized systems like the U.S. Making a trade deal with the EU is not as easy as sitting down with the EU President and hashing things out. Even if that takes place, the EU would need to vote on this deal, and if it doesn't please enough members to get a qualified majority (something the EU can find difficult on divisive issues like trade).

For those unfamiliar with EU politics, they do not have a simple two-party divide like the U.S., with their parliament currently split into nine groups, or eight plus the non-attached group.

When pressed in his own interview about the new 50% tariff rates on the EU, Treasury Sec. Scott Bessent said:

I think this is in response just to the EU’s pace.

The problem is that the EU is unlikely to be able to be motivated by the stick of tariffs to move faster, as its processes are just too slow for the Trump administration. The EU doesn't have a way to fast-track items like this through unless they have near-unanimous willpower on a subject, such as the funding of Ukraine following Russia's invasion in 2022, and even that was almost blocked by Euroskeptic Victor Orbán of Hungary.

To solve the issue, the other EU leaders asked Orbán to leave the room so they could just vote without him.

This is just an example of the inefficiencies in the European Union's governance system in regard to speed, although pro-EU forces would likely say that it is working as intended, reducing hasty decisions that aren't fully thought through, or which are motivated by the fear of getting hit with a political stick like tariffs.

Apple Gets the Stick Too

At the same time President Trump was lambasting the Europeans for not making a deal, he also took aim at Apple Inc. (AAPL) and their flagship product, the iPhone. These are traditionally made in China in factories operated by Foxconn (OTCPK:HNHPF). When the tariffs on China first came up, Apple began to accelerate its departure from China and this system, which it had been slowly decoupling from starting during Trump's first term back in 2016.

Apple was not decoupling from China in order to come back to the U.S. with its manufacturing (although it does manufacture Macs in the U.S., among some of its other products, just not iPhones), it's been going to India.

This was seen as a smart move by Apple CEO Tim Cook, as India has long had a much friendlier relationship with the U.S. geopolitically, and on trade. President Trump does not view this the same way, and instead targeted this move explicitly in a post on Truth Social.

Apple sold off on this news.

This new update indicates that the only way for Apple to mitigate a 25% charge on iPhones to the government is to manufacture them in the U.S., period. It doesn't matter what deal we make with China or India, Apple is being actively pursued here.

For the record, Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) is also being targeted by this message, though it produces far fewer phones than Apple, so it made fewer headlines.

This all begs the question: could an iPhone be built in the U.S.?

No, not without raising prices immensely. It will likely still be cheaper to pay the government 25% on every phone in import duties than it would be to move production to the U.S. Some estimates say that a U.S.-made iPhone could be triple the current price. Simple math says that 25% is less than 200%, and so Apple is likely to go with the cheaper option and take the stick head on.

U.S. labor is far more expensive than Chinese or Indian labor, and the U.S. is currently at its natural unemployment level (around 4%) meaning that there isn't a mass of unemployed folks waiting for work in manufacturing plants. Staffing and operating iPhone factories in the U.S. is essentially a non-starter for Apple, as it just doesn't make logistical sense.

This will likely harm Apple's profit margins, as they will have to eat a tax or raise prices on consumers to cover it. They are likely to, just as most businesses subject to tariffs are, meet somewhere in the middle where they pass on some costs and eat others, trying to thread the needle with supply and demand.

Conclusion

Ultimately, these latest moves by President Trump seem aimed at doing two things:

  1. Speeding up a U.S.-EU trade deal.
  2. Forcing smartphone manufacturing into the U.S. (namely Apple).

From my view, neither of these are likely to be very effective in their current implementation; while the stick has seemed to be effective in some cases, it may not be big enough in its current form to force moves from either of these two major players.

I'm not saying that I advocate for even higher tariffs and threats. We may not have needed to use the stick on our allies in the first place. Top-down planning from politicians to businesses has a poor track record, and the market will find a way to get around it if possible. Apple is fairly crafty; their response will likely not be capitulation, but they may offer some consultations to the White House. That remains to be seen.

The view of both of these entities is likely the same as many other commentators and spectators of the full tariff picture since it began on April 2nd: the rules are ever-changing as tariff rates rise and fall at will, and cannot be counted on to make long-term decisions. Even if they were going to stay for the long-term, it seems to make sense for firms like Samsung and Apple to just eat the 25%, make the treasury a little richer, and pass on what costs they can to the end-user.

The EU has its political problems to contend with, which make it very difficult to rush through policy regardless of the headwinds they are facing. To borrow a Trumpism, they just don't have the cards.

I am doubtful about the administration's progress on current trade deals, spurred on by the nebulous remarks from Treasury Sec. Bessent, who offered little guidance or specifics, but made promises that all would be revealed in due time.

I am wary of investing in markets that rely on headlines so heavily as this current one. I'm still not advocating going short, but I am still in "wait and see" mode regarding where all of this trade talk and discussion takes us.

Until July 2nd, when we really see who all has come to the table, and who hasn't, it may be best to consider risk management more heavily than normal.

As always, I repeat this line for those that still need to hear it:

Position sizing is often more important than security selection.

Thanks for reading.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Group 1 Automotive Deserves To Keep Cruising Higher
data.description:
One company that I have been consistently bullish on in recent years has been automotive retailer Group 1 Automotive, Inc. (NYSE:GPI). Back in January of this year, I decided to reaffirm the company as a Strong Buy candidate. This was based on continued attractive growth that the company was achieving and how cheap shares were on both an absolute basis and relative to similar companies. It was also in spite of the fact that, on the bottom line, things have not been going exactly great. Since then, the stock is up 1.5%, outperforming the 2.4% drop that the S&P 500 (SP500) has experienced over the same window of time. But that's nothing. Since I first rated the company a Strong Buy way back in December of 2021, shares have seen a return of 129.5%. That dwarfs the 27.3% increase that the S&P 500 has seen.

To be clear, there is a lot of uncertainty in this industry at this time. Investors would be right to be cautious. However, we don't yet know the full impact that this industry will have as a result of tariffs and what looks like an oncoming recession. Certainly, these would be negatives for the space. Having said that, management continues to grow and shares continue to trade at rather low multiples. This is true on both an absolute basis and relative to similar companies. Due to these factors, and in spite of the risks involved, I think it is too early to downgrade the company just yet.

A great business at a great price

Usually, great companies come at a high price. But sometimes, you can get a great deal on them. Over the last few years now, Group 1 Automotive has been an example of this. But even though it is a great company, this doesn't mean that it has been immune from uncertainty and weakness in its space. Take, if you will, the 2023 to 2024 fiscal years as an example. During this time, revenue for the company increased from $17.87 billion to $19.93 billion. This growth has been driven by all of the different major revenue categories for the company. The image below illustrates as much.

In that image, you can see more detailed data for the last two fiscal years. From 2023 to 2024, revenue associated with new vehicle retail sales jumped 13.7% from $8.77 billion to $9.97 billion. This was driven entirely by a 16% surge in retail new vehicles sold from 175,566 to 203,677. Things would have been better had it not been for the fact that the average sales price per new vehicle at the retail level dropped 1% from $50,325 to $49,817.

The company saw growth when it came to used vehicle retail sales as well. Revenue increased 8.5% from $5.69 billion to $6.18 billion. An 11.7% jump in used vehicles sold from 187,656 units to 209,687 units did all the work here. But as was the case with new vehicle sales, used vehicle sales also saw a drop in the average price per unit, with that figure falling 2.9% from $30,340 to $29,472. As the company expands, it sees an increase in demand for its other services as well. For instance, parts and services revenue jumped 12.1% from $2.22 billion to $2.49 billion. Meanwhile, finance and insurance sales grew 11.7% from $741.9 million to $828.7 million. You could call this ancillary revenue if you would like. At the end of the day, as the number of units the business sells increase, these revenue categories should increase as well.

Even though revenue improved, profitability did not. Net income actually dropped from $601.6 million to $498.1 million. A lot of this pain involved a decline in the gross profit per new vehicle sold from $4,369 to $3,525. That's a drop of 19.3%. There was a much more modest 1.9% decline in gross profit per used vehicle sold from $1,604 to $1,574. This might seem peculiar. However, keep in mind that, during the COVID-19 pandemic, margins for automotive retailers shot up. This was because there were supply chain shortages and some extra cash to go around. Those supply chain shortages are now a thing of the past and industry inventories have normalized.

To put this in perspective, consider that, by the end of 2024, the company had 44 days worth of supplies for new vehicles in 39 days for used vehicles. The chart above illustrates a breakdown on a consolidated basis and it also divides the data up between its US operations and its UK operations. Unfortunately, management does not provide data dating back to 2019, which would be the year before the COVID-19 pandemic arose. But we do know that, in 2020, the firm ended that year with 53 days of supplies for new vehicles and 36 days for used ones. But just one year later, in 2021, new supplies totaled just 12 days while used supplies were still pretty hefty at 42. In the US, new supplies dropped as low as 9 days. In general, used vehicles have been a lot more stable than new vehicles have been. But a lot of the bottom line benefit that the company received over the last few years came from price appreciation in both categories.

A return to more normal levels has naturally had a deleterious impact on profitability. Other profit metrics have also declined as a result. The one exception to this was operating cash flow, which skyrocketed from $190.2 million to $586.3 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $763.9 million to $666.9 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business contracted from $1.10 billion to $1.09 billion.

For the first quarter of 2025, we have seen continued growth in sales for the business. Revenue of $5.51 billion beat out the $4.47 billion reported one year earlier. Some of this was because of growth in ancillary services such as parts and services that saw revenue expand from $576.2 million to $692.1 million, and growth in finance and insurance products from $188.9 million to $226.2 million. But the real driver, as has been the case in the past, has been the rise in vehicle sales.

Year over year, new vehicle retail sales for the company jumped 22.8% from $2.18 billion to $2.68 billion. This came as the number of new vehicles sold by the company grew 26.6% from 44,302 to 56,099. There was a slight increase in the average sales price of a vehicle from $49,709 to $49,861. But sadly, the gross profit per vehicle declined from $3,601 to $3,381. That's a drop of 6.1%. Used vehicle sales grew from $1.42 billion to $1.76 billion. That increase of 23.9% can be attributed to a 21.2% rise in used vehicles sold from $49,183 to $59,618. And the average price per unit increased 2.2% from $28,806 to $29,449. Unfortunately, as was the case with new vehicles sold, the company saw a reduction in gross profit per unit from $1,669 to $1,569. That's a decline of 6% year over year.

Naturally, this decline in gross profit per vehicle caused profitability for the company to worsen. Net income contracted from $147.4 million to $127.7 million. Operating cash flow also fell from $253.9 million to $158.7 million. The good news is that, if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise in cash flow from $151.2 million to $175.5 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company increased from $266.5 million to $274.8 million.

It is important to note that this year is plagued with a particular degree of uncertainty. The tariff situation caused by the current Administration in the White House has created the concern that the number of new vehicles sold in the U.S. could drop by as many as 3.2 million this year. However, Cox Automotive did say in late April that a rush by buyers to purchase new vehicles before tariffs started reflecting in prices pushed annualized sales in March of this year to 17.8 million units. That number did drop to 16.4 million in April, but that was up from 16 million the same time one year earlier.

At the end of the day, what ultimately transpires for the year as a whole will be determined not only by tariffs, but also by whether consumers are able and willing to pay higher prices for new vehicles and used vehicles in the event that new vehicle sales do drop. A lot will depend on the days of supply that exist in the industry. If they remain more normal levels, you can fully expect automotive retailers to experience pain. But if inventories remain tight, and consumers remain somewhat resilient, the exact opposite could occur.

The good news is that management continues to make investments in growth opportunities. On May 19th, for instance, the company announced that it acquired three dealerships focused on luxury brands spread between Florida and Texas. They didn't mention what the purchase price was. But they did say that the acquired revenue should be roughly $330 million per annum. This brings the total amount of acquired revenue in 2025 up to $430 million. This followed $3.9 billion in acquired revenue that the company added in 2024.

Management also continues to buy back stock. For the year-to-date period as of the time of the May 19th release, the company repurchased 401,649 shares for a total of $167.3 million. That's massive when you consider that, for all of 2024, the company repurchased 518,465 shares in exchange for $161.6 million. In all likelihood, the company will continue to buy back stock so long as economic conditions don't worsen significantly. In fact, in November of last year, the company increased its share buyback program from the $333 million that was left under it at that time to $500 million. So I would say that additional purchases are almost certain to occur.

As for the valuation of the company, if we use the results achieved last year and the year before, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. Even with the decline in profits and cash flows in 2024, the stock looks incredibly cheap on an absolute basis. I also compared the company to five other similar firms in the table below. What I found was that, on a price to earnings basis, four of the five companies were cheaper than Group 1 Automotive. But when it came to the price to operating cash flow approach, only one of them was cheaper. And on an EV to EBITDA basis, it ended up being the cheapest of the group.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Group 1 Automotive 11.0 8.2 7.6
Sonic Automotive (SAH) 9.6 10.0 8.8
Asbury Automotive Group (ABG) 10.8 6.3 9.9
Lithia Motors (LAD) 9.7 18.1 9.1
AutoNation (AN) 10.7 20.7 9.7
Penske Automotive (PAG) 11.5 10.8 9.5

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I must say that I remain optimistic about Group 1 Automotive. Even though I sold my shares in the company quite some time ago, this was after a big increase in the share price and was because I believed that other opportunities were more promising. Even so, and even though the stock price has continued to rise, I believe that it is one of the most attractive candidates on the market. Because of this, and in spite of broader economic concerns that are creating uncertainty that investors would be wise to monitor closely, I believe that maintaining a Strong buy rating is appropriate.

data.title: Workday: Primed For A Correction As Growth Momentum Slows (Downgrade)
data.description:
Though the stock market has rallied sharply from post-tariff lows, the mood still appears to be quite nervous as the S&P 500 hovers skittishly around the ~6,000 mark. To me, the key concern is the fact that valuation multiples still remain stretched as if we are in a bull market with no macroeconomic headwinds: and yet, the Q1 earnings season so far has shown us that this couldn't be further from the truth.

Workday (NASDAQ:WDAY) is one of the more recent companies to disappoint on earnings, despite a beat-and-raise. The prominent human capital management (HCM) software company showcased a meaningful deceleration in revenue growth rates, calling into question its premium valuation multiples. The question for investors now is: can Workday rebound from here, or is this the start of a bigger correction?

I last wrote a neutral opinion on Workday in February, when the stock was still hovering in the ~$250 range. Today, Workday sits at an even higher valuation multiple, even as the company's subscription revenue growth takes its first dip into the low-teens range. Amid signals that the company's profitability margins may also be reaching a near-term apex, I'm downgrading my viewpoint on Workday to a sell rating.

To me, there are a number of major concerns with Workday that investors should be aware of:

  • Workday's product is poised for the chopping block amid macroeconomic headwinds. Companies left and right, including Workday itself, are making cuts to headcounts. That's bad news for a seat-based subscription product like Workday that primarily prices its software per head. In this environment, Workday may not only find it challenging to sign new deals, but it may also see withering revenue from within its current install base.
  • AI may also be bad news in the long run. Though Workday certainly claims AI innovation like every other software company, in the long run AI may have the impact of lowering staffing levels in many companies (or perhaps even eliminating the need for HR departments entirely when this work can be automated), pressuring the company's long-term retention.
  • Heavy dilution: We can't ignore the wide gap between the company's GAAP and pro forma operating margins, which is driven by the company's heavy annual dilution. Workday is correcting for this somewhat with its recent ~8.5% layoff announced in February, but in a more conservative market environment investors may raise more eyebrows at the company's dilution.

The cornerstone risk that I see in this stock, however, is a valuation multiple that has gradually grown richer even despite macroeconomic doldrums. At current post-earnings share prices near $255, Workday trades at a $67.68 billion market cap. When we net off the $7.97 billion of cash and $2.99 billion of debt on Workday's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $62.70 billion.

For FY26 (the year for Workday ending in January 2026), Workday has retained its full-year subscription revenue growth expectations of 14.0% y/y, while slightly upping its pro forma operating margin guidance to 28.5% (50bps higher than its previous 28.0% view). Wall Street, meanwhile, is expecting the company to generate $9.51 billion in total revenue (subscription plus professional services), or 13% y/y growth. This puts Workday's valuation at 6.6x EV/FY26 revenue.

At best, Workday is fairly valued. In my view, the stock is bound to slide down to a ~6x forward revenue multiple (closer in line with Salesforce.com) as its growth rates continue to see pressure down to the mid-teens level, and also closer to its own historical multiples.

Steer clear here and sell Workday to reinvest the proceeds in better value-oriented names.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Workday's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Workday's revenue grew 12.5% y/y to $2.24 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of $22.2 billion (+11.1% y/y) by a one-point margin. Unfortunately, Workday's growth rate also sunk considerably relative to a 15.0% growth rate in Q4: and as macro headwinds sharpen, we should expect Workday's growth to dip further as other companies reduce their headcounts and effectively reduce their subscription counts on the Workday platform.

Now to Workday's credit, the company is actively exploring new growth avenues as its share of the HCM market expands. Among the strategies that Workday is deploying is to go after vertical industries, creating more vertical-specific solutions tailored to customers in sectors like manufacturing and media, which had strong sales performance in Q1. Another growth driver for the company is going after more full-suite sales combining HCM and ERP/finance solutions, and the company notes that more than 30% of its new deals were multi-product.

Still, Workday isn't immune to macroeconomic headwinds, and though management was adamant about the macro situation not impacting Q1, it did note that certain industries such as state, local and education ("SLED") experienced a more cautious buying environment. Per CEO Carl Eschenbach's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

We did say there's a couple of industries we're keeping our eye on, specifically, SLED, as well as we're keeping our eye on the international business. Let me talk about SLED first. As you know, higher ed is always one of our strong industry verticals. And once again, that was the case in Q1. At the same time, we also know there is some headwind in that industry based on some of the grants and some of the funding they get from the federal government. So, it's just something we're keeping our eye on. It didn't necessarily have an impact on us in Q1. And there could be some adjacent impact also on healthcare if a healthcare system is part of a bigger university."

The company also mentioned that it's "keeping an eye" on performance in EMEA for potential macro impacts.

On the profitability side, at least on a pro forma basis we note that Workday's non-GAAP operating margins rose 430bps y/y to 30.2%:

The company still technically qualifies as a "Rule of 40" company, and should benefit moving forward from the layoffs of 8.5% of its headcount that it executed in February.

Still, we can't help but to point out that of the $677 million of pro forma operating income that Workday delivered in Q1, stock-based compensation (SBC) was $417 million (or roughly two-thirds) of its "profit". While it has become standard practice to remove SBC from pro forma operating metrics in the tech sector as it is a non-cash expense that has no impact on cash burn, Workday is a more mature company, and without the benefit of SBC its margin profile and its Rule of 40 status would wither. On an annualized basis, Workday's ~$1.6 billion of SBC represents ~2.5% of its market cap - which can yield a significant chunk of dilution if played out over time. This harsh reality, in my view, will do damage to Workday's valuation multiples as well.

Key takeaways

To me, Workday is at risk of a bigger correction as investors digest its lower growth rate, its substantial dilution, and the unfavorable macroeconomy hurting the company's net retention rates. I'd prefer to decamp to the sidelines here and wait for a better re-entry point before buying back in.

data.title: Diamonds In The Rough: 10 Emerging Markets Stocks To Buy Now
data.description:
Emerging Markets Rally in 2025

Grab your passport—it’s time to take a vacation from U.S. equities.

As the S&P 500 soars back to near-historic levels following heavy losses, investors are feeling optimistic that Washington’s 90-day trade truce with Beijing has helped mitigate much of the fallout from Donald Trump’s ‘Liberation Day’ tariffs. But the pain is far from over on Wall Street, as lingering trade tensions, inflation, and the threat of stagflation loom over the market. While volatile markets present plenty of opportunities to buy the dip on cheaply valued U.S. stocks with strong fundamentals, it’s also a good time to explore highly-rated stocks in economies less impacted by market uncertainty and the ongoing trade war.

A sweeping sell-off of U.S. equities, a weakening American dollar (DXY), increasing economic stimulus measures by China, and signs of bottoming earnings expectations have fueled a rare rally in emerging markets equities in 2025, with the MSCI Emerging Markets Index (EEM) up more than 10% year-to-date. Bank of America Global Research analysts see a “surprisingly positive outlook” for emerging markets equities, as they continue to benefit from a weaker dollar and low trade war exposure. The Federal Reserve’s decision to hold interest rates until the tariff impact on inflation becomes clearer could also extend that window of opportunity for EM equities, BofA’s global economist Claudio Irigoyen noted at the end of last month.

Most Emerging Markets Remain Up YTD

A weakening dollar can boost emerging market equities mainly “through three mechanisms,” asset manager AllianceBernstein writes in a market outlook:

First, a weaker dollar can attract more inflows as investors seek higher returns in a depreciating-dollar environment, potentially stimulating corporate and economic growth as well. Moreover, bond issuers benefit when the cost to service their debt drops, since many EM sovereigns and corporate bonds are denominated in US dollars. Commodities prices, a key driver across many EM economies, also tend to rise when the dollar weakens—another potential tailwind.

Not everyone is confident that those tailwinds are here to stay, however. Despite the recent rally, Wells Fargo analysts remain skeptical on emerging markets equities. They noted political instability, corporate governance concerns, and signs of a strengthening dollar as a few reasons to proceed with caution. After all, the combined market value of the three largest publicly traded American companies—Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), and Nvidia (NVDA)—continues to outweigh that of the MSCI Emerging Markets Index (EEM), which totaled around $7.9T as of April 30, 2025.

The U.S. Dollar Is Down 7.90% YTD, But Has Climbed 1.82% Over The Past Month

Investing in emerging markets is not an all-or-nothing approach, however. Selecting global stocks with excellent fundamentals and healthy balance sheets can help complement a diverse portfolio regardless of the dollar’s direction.
